title: Nonreciprocal Photon Blockade in an Asymmetric Cavity
authors: Shao-Xiong Wu, Jin-Na Fan, Dan Yan, Cheng-Hua Bai, Qiannan Wu, Mengwei Li
abstract: We propose a scheme to realize tunable and strong nonreciprocal photon blockade (PB) in an asymmetric Fabry-Pérot cavity. The setup consists of a single-mode optical cavity trapping a two-level atom, with the cavity coherently driven by a laser and the atom pumped by an auxiliary control field of the same frequency. By engineering quantum interference between multiple excitation pathways by adjusting the amplitude and relative phase of the control laser, we identify two distinct optimal control conditions that enable directional suppression of two-photon states. Under optimal control conditions, strong nonreciprocal PB is achieved, with a nonreciprocal ratio exceeding 30 dB over a broad operational bandwidth. The proposed protocol requires only standard coherent laser sources and is compatible with current cavity QED experimental setups, offering a practical and scalable platform for nonreciprocal quantum photonics.
summary: The authors propose using an asymmetric cavity QED system driven by two lasers to achieve strong nonreciprocal photon blockade. By carefully tuning the control laser's phase and amplitude, they engineer destructive quantum interference pathways. This results in robust antibunching (blockade) when light propagates forward, while observing bunching backward, demonstrating a high degree of directional control.
main_problem: The primary goal is realizing tunable and strong nonreciprocal photon blockade (PB) within an asymmetric Fabry-Pérot cavity.
main_result: Strong nonreciprocal PB, with a ratio exceeding 30 dB, is achieved by engineering quantum interference pathways using an auxiliary control laser field.
method: The analysis employs a phenomenological non-Hermitian Hamiltonian approach and utilizes the weak-driving regime approximation to solve for time evolution in a truncated Hilbert space.
model_or_system:
The system is a single-mode optical cavity trapping a two-level atom, driven by a coherent laser ($\Omega$) and controlled by an auxiliary field ($\Omega_c$). The asymmetry is inherent in the cavity decay rates ($\kappa_1 
eq \kappa_2$).
key_observables: Second-order photon correlation function ($g^{(2)}(0)$) to diagnose blockade (antibunching, $g^{(2)}<1$) and nonreciprocal ratio ($\eta = -10 \log_{10} (g^{(2)}_f(0)/g^{(2)}_b(0))$).
important_parameters: Control field amplitude ($\Omega_c$), relative phase ($	heta$), cavity decay rate ($\kappa$), and detunings ($\Delta_c, \Delta_a$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the weak-driving regime approximation ($\{\Omega, \Omega_c\} \ll \kappa$) and a truncated Hilbert space up to three excitations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the system setup, schematic pathways for quantum interference (for $\Omega_+^c$ vs $\Omega_-^c$), $g^{(2)}(0)$ dependence on control parameters, and time-delayed correlation functions ($g^{(2)}(	au)$) showing distinct forward (PB) and backward (bunching) behavior.
paper_structure: The paper develops the non-Hermitian Hamiltonian model, applies weak-driving approximations to derive coupled differential equations for probability amplitudes, solves for steady-state observables like $g^{(2)}(0)$, and finally compares two optimal control conditions ($\Omega_+^c$ vs $\Omega_-^c$) to quantify the resulting nonreciprocity.
why_interesting: This work provides a highly practical theoretical blueprint for generating directional quantum light sources, which is crucial for advancing quantum networking and measurement protocols that require time-reversal symmetry breaking.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic of the system. A two-level atom is trapped inside an asymmetric Fabry-P´erot cavity, the cavity is driven by a coher- ent laser and the atom is pumped by a control laser with the same frequency. When the driving laser is injected from the left mirror toward the right, it is deﬁned as the forward case, and the backward case corresponds to the opposite propagating direction. By adjust- ing the control laser, the photons exhibit photon antibunching in the forward case and bunching behaviour in the backward case, i.e., the nonreciprocal PB effect.

FIG. 2: The second-order correlation function g(2)(0) and the mean intracavity photon number ⟨a†a⟩as functions of the atomic detuning ∆a. Panels (a) and (c) correspond to the optimal control condition Ω+ c , while panels (b) and (d) represent the optimal control condition Ω− c . Red solid lines and blue dashed lines denote the analytical results derived via the probability amplitude method for the forward and backward cases, respectively. Black circles o and asterisks ∗mean the corresponding numerical solutions obtained from the quantum master equation.

FIG. 3: Schematic of the energy-level structure and the transition pathways. The blue arrows denote transitions induced by the driving ﬁeld, while the red/green arrow represents the pumping by the con- trol condition Ω+ c or Ω− c .

FIG. 4: The population of two-photon state |2g⟩as a function of the atomic detuning ∆a. Panel (a) corresponds to the optimal control condition Ω+ c , and panel (b) to Ω− c control. The parameters are cho- sen those in Fig. 2.

FIG. 5: The nonreciprocal ratio η as a function of the atomic detuning ∆a. Panel (a) corresponds to the optimal control condition Ω+ c , and panel (b) to Ω− c control. The light-blue regions indicate that η > 30 dB. The parameters are chosen those in Fig. 2.
